title: Introduction to HTML
summary: Learn the core HTML tags that structure every web page — from head and body to sections, lists, and semantic layout.

guide.title: Before you start — set up on your computer
guide.intro: Do these steps once before Example 1. You will save your own HTML file on the Desktop and open it in a browser.

steps.title: Create a folder on your Desktop.
steps.text: Right-click on an empty area of your Desktop → New → Folder. Name it something easy to find, for example My Website.

steps.title: Create an HTML file inside the folder.
steps.text: Open the folder. Right-click inside → New → Text Document. Rename the file to index.html (type the full name including .html). If Windows asks “Are you sure you want to change the extension?” click Yes.

steps.title: Open the file in your browser.
steps.text: Double-click index.html. It may open as a blank page — that is normal for now.

steps.title: Open the same file in a text editor.
steps.text: Right-click index.html → Open with → Notepad (or use VS Code if you have it). Keep the editor open so you can paste code from the examples below.

steps.title: Get ready for Example 1.
steps.text: Scroll down to The head element. Read each example, then try building a complete page in your index.html with <!DOCTYPE html>, <html>, <head>, and <body>. Save (Ctrl+S) and refresh the browser to see changes.

guide.footer: When your folder, index.html, and text editor are ready, continue to Example 1 below.

examples.title: The head element
examples.description: The <head> element holds information about the page that is not shown on screen — charset, title, and links to CSS. Every HTML page needs exactly one <head> inside <html>. Add <meta name="description" content="My first page"> inside the head in your own file.

files.html:
This text is in the body. The <head> is invisible here but sets charset and page title for the browser.

Open your browser tab title — that comes from <title> inside <head>.

examples.title: The title element
examples.description: The <title> element sets the name shown in the browser tab and in bookmarks. Put one <title> inside <head> on every page. Change the title text and check the browser tab after you refresh.

files.html:
Page heading (visible)

The <title> tag does not appear on the page — it only names the tab.

Example title for this lesson: Introduction to HTML

examples.title: The body element
examples.description: The <body> element contains everything visitors see — text, images, links, and sections. Use one <body> per page, after <head>. Add a second paragraph below the first one.

files.html:
Welcome to my page

Everything you see here lives inside the <body> element.

Headings, paragraphs, images, and forms all go in the body.
files.css:
body { font-family: Georgia, serif; padding: 1.5rem; background: #fafafa; max-width: 40rem; margin: 0 auto; }
h1 { color: #1e40af; }

examples.title: The header element
examples.description: The <header> element groups introductory content at the top — logo, site name, or opening banner. Use at the top of a page or inside an <article>. Change the site name to your own name.

files.html:
Student Learning Hub

HTML Lesson 1 — Introduction

Content below the header starts here.

examples.title: The footer element
examples.description: The <footer> element holds closing information — copyright, contact, or links. Place at the bottom of a page or article. Add your email or school name in the footer.

files.html:
Lesson content

Main content appears above the footer.

© 2026 Taufiq Chin — All rights reserved

Last updated: June 2026

examples.title: The section element
examples.description: The <section> element groups related content under one theme, usually with its own heading. Use when a block of content has a clear topic (e.g. “About”, “Gallery”). Add a second <section> with a different heading.

files.html:
What is HTML?

HTML stands for HyperText Markup Language. It describes the structure of a web page.

Why learn HTML?

Every website uses HTML. It is the foundation of web development.

examples.title: The p element
examples.description:
The

element defines a paragraph of text. Wrap each normal block of text in its own

. Browsers add space between paragraphs automatically. Add a third paragraph with your own sentence.

files.html:
My study notes

HTML uses tags written in angle brackets.

Most tags come in pairs: an opening tag and a closing tag.

Paragraphs help readers follow your ideas step by step.
files.css:
body { font-family: Georgia, serif; padding: 1.5rem; line-height: 1.7; max-width: 34rem; }
h2 { color: #1e293b; }

examples.title: The div element
examples.description: The <div> is a generic block container with no special meaning — used to group elements for layout or styling. Use when no semantic tag (<section>, <article>, etc.) fits better. Change the background color of one box in the CSS panel.

files.html:
Box A — first group
Box B — second group
Box C — third group

examples.title: The span element
examples.description: The <span> is an inline container — it styles part of a sentence without starting a new line. Highlight a word or phrase inside a paragraph. Wrap another word in <span class="highlight">.

files.html:
HTML has block elements like div and inline elements like span.

Inline elements flow with the text on the same line.

examples.title: The nav element
examples.description: The <nav> element groups major navigation links — menus that help users move around the site. Use for main menus, not for every random link. Add a third link called “Lessons”.

files.html:
My Website

Home About Contact

Click the links above (they are placeholders using #).

examples.title: Lists: ul, ol, and li
examples.description: <ul> makes a bullet list, <ol> makes a numbered list, and <li> is each list item. Use ul for unordered steps or features; use ol when order matters (1, 2, 3). Add one more item to each list.

files.html:
Tools I use

  • VS Code editor
  • Chrome browser
  • GitHub for saving code

Steps to publish a page

  1. Write HTML
  2. Save the file
  3. Upload to GitHub Pages

examples.title: HTML comments
examples.description: Comments use <!-- text --> and are ignored by the browser — they are notes for you and other developers. Explain tricky code or temporarily hide markup while testing. Add your own comment above the heading.

files.html:
Visible heading

Comments do not appear on the page. They help you remember what each section does.
files.css:
body { font-family: system-ui, sans-serif; padding: 1.5rem; max-width: 34rem; line-height: 1.6; }
h2 { color: #059669; margin-top: 0; }

examples.title: The article element
examples.description: The <article> element wraps self-contained content that could stand alone — a blog post, news item, or card. Each article should make sense if copied to another page. Change the article title and author name.

files.html:
How I learned HTML

By Aina · 5 min read

I started with small examples, ran them in the browser, and changed one tag at a time.

examples.title: The aside element
examples.description: The <aside> element holds content related to the main topic but not the main focus — tips, links, or ads. Sidebars, pull quotes, or “Did you know?” boxes. Add a second tip inside the aside.

files.html:
Main lesson

This is the primary content students should read first.

Tip

Save your file often with Ctrl+S while learning.

examples.title: The embed element
examples.description: The <embed> element brings external content into your page — such as a PDF or plugin content. Use for embedded documents; for videos, <video> is often better. Change the width value and see how the box resizes.

files.html:
Embedded PDF sample

Below is an embedded document using <embed>:

examples.title: Putting it together
examples.description: Real pages combine header, nav, section, article, aside, and footer into one layout. Use this pattern for portfolios, lesson pages, and blogs. Add a new link in nav or a second article card.

files.html:
Web Dev Notes

Home | Lessons | About

Lesson 1 complete

You now know the basic structural tags of HTML.

Next step

Move on to images, links, and multimedia in Lesson 2.

© 2026 — Keep practicing!

practice.description: Build a page with header, nav (at least 2 links), one section, one article, an aside, a footer, and a ul list with 3 items.

starter.html:
<!DOCTYPE html>
<html lang="en">
<head>
  <meta charset="UTF-8">
  <meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width, initial-scale=1.0">
  <title>My HTML Practice Page</title>
</head>
<body>
  <!-- header -->
  <!-- nav -->
  <!-- section with article -->
  <!-- aside -->
  <!-- ul list -->
  <!-- footer -->
</body>
</html>

hints: Comments <!-- --> can help you plan each part
